children.name: Apus
children.description: Masters of aerial life — swifts sleep, eat, drink, and even mate on the wing, landing only to nest. Common swifts arrive in Swedish towns in May and leave in July, spending the rest of the year airborne over Africa. Their screaming parties around rooftops on summer evenings are one of the defining sounds of Swedish cities.

children.name: Apus apus
children.description: Spends almost its entire life airborne, even sleeping on the wing at high altitude, and touches down only to breed in roof cavities. Its screaming summer-evening flights around town rooftops are among the most evocative sounds of the Swedish city.

children.name: Apus pallidus
children.description: A paler, browner relative of the Common Swift with a slightly broader-winged, more relaxed flight, breeding around the Mediterranean. A rare vagrant to Sweden, mainly recorded in autumn.

children.name: Apus nipalensis
children.description: A small swift of South and Southeast Asia that nests colonially under building eaves and rock overhangs. Has a conspicuous white throat and rump band.

children.name: Apus affinis
children.description: A small, square-tailed swift with a bold white rump, increasingly colonising southern Europe. Nests colonially under bridges and buildings.

children.name: Apus caffer
children.description: A slender, deeply forked swift with a narrow white rump crescent, breeding in old swallow nests. Has expanded into Spain and Morocco.

children.name: Apus horus
children.description: An African swift with a broad white rump that nests in burrows excavated by bee-eaters and martins. Named after the Egyptian sky god Horus.

children.name: Apus barbatus
children.description: A large dark swift of African mountains and cliffs, similar to the Common Swift but with a paler throat. Breeds on inaccessible rock faces.

children.name: Apus unicolor
children.description: A slim, uniformly dark swift endemic as a breeder to the Macaronesian islands of Madeira and the Canaries. Some birds winter in West Africa.

children.name: Apus acuticauda
children.description: A little-known swift of the eastern Himalayas with a needle-pointed tail, breeding on sheer cliffs. IUCN Vulnerable owing to a small, fragmented range.

children.name: Apus pacificus
children.description: A long-distance migratory swift breeding across eastern Asia and wintering to Australia, with a white rump band and deeply forked tail. A rare vagrant to Europe and North America.

children.name: Apus berliozi
children.description: A swift of the Horn of Africa and Socotra with a pale throat and rump, breeding on coastal cliffs and islands.

children.name: Tachymarptis
children.description: Large, powerful swifts with white bellies.

children.name: Tachymarptis melba
children.description: A large, powerful swift with a white belly and brown breast-band, breeding on cliffs and tall buildings in southern Europe. A scarce overshoot vagrant to Sweden, where its sheer size and slow wingbeats stand out.

children.name: Tachymarptis aequatorialis
children.description: A large African swift with a mottled grey underbody, soaring over highlands and gorges. Closely related to the Alpine Swift.

children.name: Chaetura
children.description: Spinetailed swifts of the Americas with stiff, spine-tipped tail feathers used to brace against vertical surfaces; many nest in hollow trees and chimneys.

children.name: Chaetura pelagica
children.description: A cigar-shaped North American swift that nests and roosts in chimneys, migrating to the western Amazon. IUCN Vulnerable due to steep population declines.

children.name: Chaetura vauxi
children.description: The smallest North American swift, nesting in hollow trees of western forests and famous for spectacular communal roosts in chimneys during migration.

children.name: Chaetura brachyura
children.description: A compact, pale-rumped swift of the Caribbean and northern South America with a very short tail, often seen low over towns and forest.

children.name: Chaetura cinereiventris
children.description: A small dark swift with a contrasting grey rump, ranging from Nicaragua through tropical South America over forest and clearings.

children.name: Chaetura spinicauda
children.description: A small spinetail swift of northern South America with a narrow pale rump band, foraging over forest and clearings.

children.name: Chaetura chapmani
children.description: A dark, glossy spinetail swift of tropical South America, nesting in hollow trees and chimneys.

children.name: Cypseloides
children.description: Large, dark swifts of the Americas that nest in caves and behind waterfalls; among the most aerial of birds.

children.name: Cypseloides niger
children.description: A large dark swift of western North America that nests behind waterfalls and on sea cliffs, wintering in Amazonian Brazil. Notoriously hard to study.

children.name: Cypseloides cryptus
children.description: A poorly known dark swift of the Neotropics with a small white chin patch, nesting near waterfalls in mountainous terrain.

children.name: Cypseloides fumigatus
children.description: A medium-sized dark swift of southeastern South America that nests behind and beside waterfalls in forested highlands.

children.name: Streptoprocne
children.description: Large, powerful swifts of the Neotropics, often with white collars, that breed in caves and gorges and forage over huge areas.

children.name: Streptoprocne zonaris
children.description: A very large swift with a bold white collar, ranging from Mexico to Argentina. Forms large flocks foraging high over mountains and lowlands.

children.name: Streptoprocne rutila
children.description: A medium-large swift with a rufous collar in males, nesting in caves and behind waterfalls in the Neotropical highlands.

children.name: Streptoprocne biscutata
children.description: A large swift of eastern and central South America with two white patches at the neck sides, breeding colonially in caves and canyons.

children.name: Streptoprocne semicollaris
children.description: A very large swift endemic to the mountains of Mexico, with a white nape patch, breeding in deep caves and volcanic chasms.

children.name: Aerodramus
children.description: Echolocating swiftlets of the Indo-Pacific that nest in dark caves; the saliva nests of several species are harvested for bird's-nest soup.

children.name: Aerodramus fuciphagus
children.description: A cave-nesting swiftlet whose nest is built almost entirely of hardened saliva and is the prime ingredient of bird's-nest soup, supporting a major industry.

children.name: Aerodramus maximus
children.description: A swiftlet that builds its nest from saliva mixed with feathers, harvested as the lower-grade 'black nest'. Uses echolocation to navigate cave colonies.

children.name: Aerodramus spodiopygius
children.description: A small echolocating swiftlet of the southwest Pacific and northeastern Australia, nesting deep in caves in large colonies.

children.name: Aerodramus vanikorensis
children.description: A widespread cave-nesting swiftlet of island Southeast Asia and Melanesia that echolocates in darkness, with plain sooty plumage.

children.name: Aerodramus bartschi
children.description: An echolocating swiftlet endemic to the Mariana Islands, IUCN Endangered after declines from habitat loss and the invasive brown tree snake.

children.name: Aerodramus brevirostris
children.description: An echolocating swiftlet of Himalayan and Southeast Asian mountains, nesting in caves and gorges and descending to lowlands in winter.

children.name: Aerodramus salangana
children.description: A cave swiftlet of Borneo and nearby islands that builds nests of moss bound with saliva, echolocating in deep cave darkness.

children.name: Collocalia
children.description: Small glossy swiftlets of Asia and the Pacific; unlike Aerodramus, most do not echolocate.

children.name: Collocalia esculenta
children.description: A tiny glossy blue-black swiftlet with a white belly, nesting in caves, buildings and culverts across the Indo-Pacific. Often seen in fast low flocks.

children.name: Collocalia linchi
children.description: A small dark swiftlet of Java, Bali and Sumatra with a greyish belly, building moss-and-saliva nests in caves and under eaves.

children.name: Collocalia troglodytes
children.description: The world's smallest swift, endemic to the Philippines, nesting in caves and crevices. Tiny and glossy with a faint pale rump.

children.name: Hirundapus
children.description: Needletails — the fastest swifts in level flight, powerful large-bodied birds with spine-tipped tails.

children.name: Hirundapus caudacutus
children.description: A very large, powerful swift reputed to be among the fastest birds in flapping flight. Breeds in Asia and migrates to Australia; a celebrated vagrant in Europe.

children.name: Hirundapus giganteus
children.description: A large, fast needletail of forested South and Southeast Asia with a white throat and undertail, hawking insects high above the canopy.

children.name: Cypsiurus
children.description: Palm swifts — slender, long-tailed swifts that glue their nests to the underside of palm fronds.

children.name: Cypsiurus parvus
children.description: A slim, long forked-tailed swift of African palm savanna that builds a tiny nest of feathers and saliva on a vertical palm frond, gluing the eggs in place.

children.name: Cypsiurus balasiensis
children.description: A small, slender Asian swift closely tied to fan palms, attaching its pad-like nest to the underside of fronds. Fast and agile in flight.

children.name: Tachornis
children.description: Small Neotropical palm swifts associated with palms and feather-pad nests.

children.name: Tachornis squamata
children.description: A small, slender swift of Amazonian palm groves with a deeply forked tail, nesting in dead hanging palm leaves.

children.name: Tachornis phoenicobia
children.description: A small swift endemic to the Greater Antilles, nesting colonially in the dead skirts of palms. Abundant over Caribbean towns and countryside.

children.name: Panyptila
children.description: Swallow-tailed swifts of the Americas that build long, tubular hanging nests of plant down and saliva.

children.name: Panyptila sanctihieronymi
children.description: A large, deeply fork-tailed black-and-white swift of montane Central America that builds a remarkable long tubular nest hanging from cliffs.

children.name: Panyptila cayennensis
children.description: A small pied swift with a forked tail, ranging from southern Mexico to Brazil, that builds an elongated felt-like hanging nest tube.

children.name: Telacanthura
children.description: Large African spinetail swifts with white throats and rumps that nest in hollow trees.

children.name: Telacanthura usheri
children.description: An African spinetail swift with a white throat and rump band, nesting in hollow baobabs and other large trees. Spine-tipped tail aids clinging.

children.name: Raphidura
children.description: Small spinetail swifts with silvery-white rumps.

children.name: Raphidura sabini
children.description: A small, glossy black spinetail swift with a brilliant white rump, hawking insects above the canopy of West and Central African rainforest.
